questions.question: Explain the difference between Feature Scaling and Feature Selection. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Feature Scaling transforms existing features to similar scales (e.g., Min-Max, Standardization)
Feature Selection chooses a subset of relevant features from all available features
Scaling doesn't reduce dimensionality, Selection reduces number of features
Example Scaling: Standardization for KNN
Example Selection: Variance Threshold or LASSO

questions.question: When is Min-Max Scaling preferred over Standardization? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Min-Max is preferred when feature values have known bounded ranges
Used for algorithms sensitive to specific ranges like neural networks
Example: Image pixel values (0-255) or percentages (0-100)
Standardization is better for normally distributed data and distance-based algorithms
Min-Max is sensitive to outliers unlike Standardization

questions.question: Differentiate between Filter, Wrapper, and Embedded methods of Feature Selection.
keyPoints: Filter Methods: Evaluate features independently using statistical tests (correlation, chi-square, mutual information) - fast but ignore model interactions
Wrapper Methods: Use a predictive model to evaluate feature subsets (RFE, Forward/Backward selection) - accurate but computationally expensive
Embedded Methods: Perform selection during model training (LASSO, tree importance) - balance of speed and accuracy
Filter is model-agnostic, Wrapper and Embedded are model-dependent

questions.question: What is Multicollinearity? How does it affect feature selection and how is it handled?
keyPoints: Multicollinearity exists when two or more features are highly correlated
Causes redundancy, unstable model coefficients, and inflated variance
Detected using Correlation Matrix or VIF (Variance Inflation Factor)
Handled by removing one of highly correlated features (typically correlation > 0.7-0.9
Can also use PCA or regularization (Ridge/LASSO) to address multicollinearity

questions.question: How does LASSO Regression perform automatic Feature Selection?
keyPoints: LASSO uses L1 regularization adding penalty λ∑|w| to loss function
The L1 penalty can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero
Features with zero coefficients are effectively eliminated from the model
This provides interpretable model with only relevant features
Elastic Net combines L1 and L2 for better feature selection with correlated features

questions.question: Explain the curse of dimensionality and how Feature Selection helps address it.
keyPoints: As dimensions increase, volume of space grows exponentially making data sparse
Sparse data leads to overfitting, increased computational cost, and unreliable distance metrics
Feature Selection reduces dimensionality by removing irrelevant/redundant features
Helps improve model generalization, reduce training time, and enhance interpretability
Prevents overfitting especially when number of samples is much less than number of features

questions.question: What is Robust Scaling? When and why would you use it instead of other scaling methods?
keyPoints: Robust Scaling uses median and Interquartile Range (IQR) instead of mean and standard deviation
Formula: X_scaled = (X - median) / IQR
Preferred when data contains outliers or extreme values
Unlike Min-Max and Standardization, it is not affected by outliers
Common in scenarios where data quality is uncertain or outliers are meaningful

questions.question: Explain Min-Max Scaling and Standardization (Z-score) with their formulas, advantages, disadvantages, and when to use each method. Provide a comparison table.
keyPoints: Min-Max: X_scaled = (X-X_min)/(X_max-X_min), scales to [0,1], sensitive to outliers, preserves distribution shape
Standardization: X_scaled = (X-μ)/σ, mean=0, std=1, handles outliers better, changes distribution to normal
Min-Max use cases: bounded ranges (images, percentages), neural networks requiring bounded inputs
Standardization use cases: distance-based algorithms (KNN, SVM, K-Means), PCA, normally distributed data
Comparison: Min-Max bounded but outlier-sensitive; Standardization unbounded but outlier-robust
Both are linear transformations preserving relationships if monotonic

questions.question: Describe Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) in detail. Explain how it works, its algorithm steps, advantages, limitations, and write pseudocode for the same.
keyPoints: RFE is a wrapper method using backward elimination approach
Algorithm: 1) Train model on all features 2) Rank features by importance 3) Remove least important 4) Repeat until desired number reached
Uses model coefficients (linear) or feature importance (tree-based) for ranking
Advantages: considers feature interactions, accurate selection, works with any model
Limitations: computationally expensive, may not find optimal subset, sensitive to initial model
Time complexity: O(n² × m) where n=features, m=iterations

questions.question: A dataset has 1000 features and only 100 samples. Explain the problems that arise and propose a complete Feature Selection pipeline to address them.
keyPoints: Problem 1: Curse of dimensionality - sparse data in 1000D space
Problem 2: Overfitting - model memorizes noise with more features than samples
Problem 3: Increased computational cost and memory requirements
Problem 4: Multicollinearity likely among many features
Pipeline Step 1: Remove constant/quasi-constant features using Variance Threshold
Pipeline Step 2: Remove highly correlated features using correlation matrix (>0.9)
Pipeline Step 3: Use Filter method (ANOVA F-test or Mutual Information) for initial ranking
Pipeline Step 4: Apply Embedded method (LASSO) for automatic selection
Pipeline Step 5: Use Cross-Validation with Wrapper method (RFE) for final selection
Alternative: Consider PCA if interpretation not required

questions.question: Write Python code to perform feature scaling using StandardScaler and feature selection using SelectKBest with f_classif on the iris dataset. Explain each step.
keyPoints: Import necessary libraries: sklearn datasets, StandardScaler, SelectKBest, f_classif
Load iris dataset using load_iris()
Separate features (X) and target (y)
Apply StandardScaler: scaler = StandardScaler(); X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)
Apply SelectKBest: selector = SelectKBest(score_func=f_classif, k=2); X_selected = selector.fit_transform(X_scaled, y)
Explain fit_transform vs transform pattern
Show how to get selected feature indices and scores
Explain that k=2 selects top 2 features based on F-scores
Demonstrate inverse_transform to see selected features
